name: Raghavendra Tirtha
dates_notes: Maṭha records, royal inscriptions, near-contemporary hagiography.

dates_evidence.description: Royal inscriptions in the Mantrālaya region.
Mādhva maṭha records and near-contemporary hagiography.

core_thesis: Rāghavendra Tīrtha is the late-classical synthesiser of Mādhva scholasticism, the figure through whom the school's commentarial stack reaches its definitive form. His doctrinal commitments are wholly continuous with Madhva, Jayatīrtha, and Vyāsatīrtha — *svatantra-paratantra* taratamya, *pañca-bheda*, *viśeṣa* as the device of intra-substantial differentiation, *bimba-pratibimba* as the structure of *jīva*-Viṣṇu dependence, *sākṣi* as a self-luminous *pramāṇa*, the rejection of *mithyātva* under all five *lakṣaṇas* — and his contribution is the writing of *prakāśa*-commentaries ("illuminations") on virtually every layer of the prior tradition, weaving them into a single mutually-consistent reading. He is also a major devotional theologian: alongside the technical *prakāśa*-corpus he composed the *Daśa-Avatāra-Stuti* and many other *stotras* in which the *Tattvavāda* metaphysics finds its liturgical voice. The *bhakti-yoga* of *Tattvavāda* — Viṣṇu as *svatantra*, *jīva* as ontologically subordinate but eternally individual, *aparokṣa-jñāna* mediated by graded *bhakti* — receives in Rāghavendra both its most refined scholastic articulation and its most fully developed devotional grammar.

engaged_works.title: Tantra-Dipika
engaged_works.summary: The *Tantra-Dīpikā* is Rāghavendra's *prakāśa*-style commentary on the *Anuvyākhyāna* (Madhva's verse-*vārttika* on the *Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya*), conducted through and in dialogue with Jayatīrtha's *Nyāya-Sudhā* — that is, it is a third-order commentary mediating Madhva and Jayatīrtha. Working *kārikā* by *kārikā*, Rāghavendra fixes the school's reading of the *Anuvyākhyāna* in light of intervening polemic: where Vijayīndra and others have answered Appayya- and Madhusūdana-line objections, the *Tantra-Dīpikā* records the resolved Mādhva position and integrates it into the commentarial flow. The text is especially valuable for its treatments of the *bheda*-arguments at the opening of *Anuvyākhyāna* 2, the *viśeṣa*-discussion, and the *bimba-pratibimba* arguments concerning the *jīva*'s relation to Viṣṇu. The *Tantra-Dīpikā* is the canonical late-medieval companion to the *Nyāya-Sudhā* and remains the obligatory point of reference for any modern Mādhva exegesis of the *Anuvyākhyāna*.

engaged_works.title: Nyaya-Sudha-Parimala
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored sub-sub-commentary on Jayatīrtha's Nyāya-Sudhā.
engaged_works.summary: The *Pariṃala* ("Fragrance") is Rāghavendra's sub-sub-commentary on Jayatīrtha's *Nyāya-Sudhā* — a fourth-order text on Madhva's *Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya*, composed in *Navya-Nyāya* idiom inherited from Vyāsatīrtha. Where Jayatīrtha had reconstructed each *Anuvyākhyāna*-argument in formal *anumāna*-form, Rāghavendra in the *Pariṃala* both records the school's settled response to objections developed in the intervening centuries (Appayya, Madhusūdana, Brahmānanda, the Vivaraṇa-line) and refines the technical apparatus where contemporary *Navya-Nyāya* had moved on. It is the most demanding and most complete of the late-Mādhva commentarial productions, and standardly considered the high-water mark of Mādhva scholasticism on the *Anuvyākhyāna*-*Sudhā* axis. (Rāghavendra's parallel *Pariṃala* on Vyāsatīrtha's *Tarka-Tāṇḍava* — sometimes counted as a separate work, sometimes grouped with this — performs the same function for the Naiyāyika engagement.) The *Pariṃala* is the natural terminus of the Madhva → Jayatīrtha → Vyāsatīrtha → Rāghavendra commentarial cascade.

engaged_works.title: Tarka-Tandava-Parimala
engaged_works.composition_dates_notes: Composed in Rāghavendra's mature period as the school's response to the developing Navya-Nyāya scholasticism.
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored Pariṃala on Vyāsatīrtha's Tarka-Tāṇḍava.
engaged_works.summary: The *Pariṃala* on Vyāsatīrtha's *Tarka-Tāṇḍava* — the *Navya-Dvaita* engagement with Naiyāyika epistemology and dialectic, paralleling on the *tarka*-front what the *Nyāyāmṛta* accomplishes on the Advaita-front. Where the *Nyāya-Sudhā-Pariṃala* refines the school's *Anuvyākhyāna* exegesis in *Navya-Nyāya* idiom, the *Tarka-Tāṇḍava-Pariṃala* engages directly with Gaṅgeśa-line Navya-Nyāya itself: Rāghavendra works through Vyāsatīrtha's section-by-section critique of Naiyāyika doctrines (the analysis of *vyāpti*, the *paramārśa*-structure of *anumāna*, the treatment of *upādhi*, the analysis of *kevalānvayī* and *kevala-vyatirekī* inferences, the *prāmāṇya-vāda*) and brings the school's response up to date with the further developments in Navya-Nyāya since Vyāsatīrtha's composition (especially through Raghunātha Śiromaṇi and Gadādhara). The text is the late-Mādhva school's definitive engagement with Navya-Nyāya epistemology and the obligatory reference for any Mādhva *tarka*-shastraic work in the modern period. Together with the *Nyāya-Sudhā-Pariṃala* it completes Rāghavendra's *Pariṃala*-corpus on the school's two principal scholastic fronts.

engaged_works.title: Tatparya-Dipika
engaged_works.summary: The *Tātparya-Dīpikā* is Rāghavendra's *prakāśa* on Vyāsatīrtha's *Tātparya-Candrikā* — and so a fifth-order text on the *Brahma-Sūtra* (*Sūtra* → Madhva *Bhāṣya* → Jayatīrtha *Tattva-Prakāśikā* → Vyāsatīrtha *Tātparya-Candrikā* → Rāghavendra *Tātparya-Dīpikā*). The work documents the school's settled construal of every contested *adhikaraṇa* of the *Sūtra* in light of the full polemical history through the early seventeenth century; it functions both as a final running commentary on the *Sūtra*-text and as a *digest* of the prior commentarial layers. Particular attention is given to *adhikaraṇa*s where Vyāsatīrtha had introduced *Navya-Nyāya* refinements (the *ānanda-mayādhikaraṇa*, the *jīva-pratibimba* discussion at *BS* 2.3.43–53, the *bheda*-*sūtras* of *adhyāya* 4): Rāghavendra both glosses the refinements and shows how they cohere with Jayatīrtha and Madhva. Together with the *Pariṃala* and *Tantra-Dīpikā*, the *Tātparya-Dīpikā* completes the late-Mādhva commentarial architecture on the *Brahma-Sūtra*.

engaged_works.title: Multiple Prakasha sub-commentaries
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored sub-commentary corpus on the Madhva–Jayatīrtha tradition.
engaged_works.summary: Beyond the principal *Tantra-Dīpikā*, *Pariṃala*, and *Tātparya-Dīpikā*, Rāghavendra composed a wider corpus of *prakāśa*-style sub-commentaries that together comment on virtually every layer of the prior Mādhva tradition. These include glosses on Madhva's *Daśa-Prakaraṇa* texts (the *Tattvodyota*, *Māyāvāda-Khaṇḍana*, *Mithyātvānumāna-Khaṇḍana*, and *Upādhi-Khaṇḍana* triad), on the *Viṣṇu-Tattva-Vinirṇaya* and *Karma-Nirṇaya*, on the *Gītā-Bhāṣya* and *Gītā-Tātparya-Nirṇaya*, on portions of the *Bhāgavata-Tātparya-Nirṇaya* and *Mahābhārata-Tātparya-Nirṇaya*, and on several of Jayatīrtha's smaller *ṭīkās*. The unifying intent is to bring every Mādhva text into a single integrated reading consistent with the *Tantra-Dīpikā*–*Pariṃala*–*Tātparya-Dīpikā* spine. Rāghavendra's *prakāśa*-corpus, read together with his *stotra*-literature and his pontifical-pedagogical role, is the principal vehicle through which *Tattvavāda* enters its modern reception; almost every twentieth-century Dvaita exegesis works downstream of these glosses.
